ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Japanese pickled vegetables; SNOMED CT coverage for traditional Asian condiment foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of Japanese pickled vegetables is approximately 30g, served as a small condiment or palate cleanser alongside a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Vegetables, pickled, mixed (SR Legacy #11979 / closest analogous composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cites HPB Singapore as the data source with energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7g, fat 0.0g, carbohydrate 2.36g per 100g. The protein value of 10.7g/100g is anomalously high for pickled vegetables (typical range 0.5–2.0g/100g) and is likely a data entry error in the source record. However, per Project Veritas policy, the HPB-sourced values are preserved as-is from the canonical source and documented in notes. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mixed pickled vegetables (NDB 11979) reports energy ~17 kcal, protein ~0.9g, fat ~0.1g, carbohydrate ~3.5g per 100g; sodium ~800mg/100g. Secondary nutrient values (sodium, fibre, sugar, saturated fat) are drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the closest approved source for pickled vegetable composites, given that HPB did not report these fields. Fat_g is 0.0 per HPB source — confirmed zero per HPB record; this is plausible for vinegar-pickled vegetables with no oil added.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on pickled/fermented vegetables with vinegar
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Japanese tsukemono in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on vinegar-pickled mixed vegetables; vinegar-based pickling is well-documented to reduce postprandial glycaemic response (GI suppression effect of acetic acid). Carbohydrate content is very low (2.36g/100g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.2 per 30g serving, which is clinically negligible. Estimate is conservative and defensible per approved GI source methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~800mg/100g) from salt-curing and brine
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should strictly limit tsukemono to a small garnish portion (10–15g per meal) due to sodium content of approximately 800mg/100g, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium load and raise blood pressure; low-sodium or vinegar-only preparations are preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.36g/100g) and vinegar-mediated glycaemic suppression
type2_diabetes.guidance: Japanese pickled vegetables are suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to their very low carbohydrate content (~2.4g/100g) and the acetic acid in vinegar-based pickling, which attenuates postprandial blood glucose rise; however, patients should avoid sweet pickles with added sugar and monitor sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content raises cardiovascular risk despite negligible fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tsukemono contains negligible fat and no cholesterol, the high sodium content (~800mg/100g) poses a cardiovascular risk by contributing to hypertension; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portions to 10–15g and favour low-sodium or vinegar-only preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic benefit from fermentation versus sodium-driven tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fermented tsukemono with live cultures may support gut microbiome health and modulate immune-mediated inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis, but high sodium content (>600mg/100g) may worsen joint swelling; limit to small portions and avoid nightshade-based pickles if individual sensitivity is noted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese pickled vegetables, known as tsukemono (漬物), are a traditional Japanese condiment and side dish prepared by preserving vegetables in salt, vinegar, rice bran (nukadoko), miso, sake lees, or soy sauce. Common vegetables used include daikon radish, cucumber, eggplant, cabbage, ginger, and plum (umeboshi). Tsukemono are served as a palate cleanser, digestive aid, and flavour contrast alongside rice, miso soup, and main dishes in traditional Japanese meals. They are characteristically crunchy, tangy, salty, and sometimes slightly sour or umami-rich depending on the pickling medium. Nutritionally, tsukemono are very low in carbohydrates (~2.4g/100g) and fat (0g/100g), making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic diets in small portions. However, they are very high in sodium (~800mg/100g) due to the salt-curing process, which is a significant concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Fermented varieties with live cultures may provide probiotic benefit. The glycaemic index is low (~32), and the glycaemic load per standard 30g serving is negligible (~0.2). Tsukemono are a staple of Japanese cuisine and are widely consumed in Singapore as part of Japanese restaurant meals.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in pickled vegetables?
Can I eat pickled vegetables if I have high blood pressure?
What are Japanese pickles made of?
What is the GI of pickled vegetables?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, fat, carbohydrate) are sourced from HPB Singapore and are broadly plausible for pickled vegetables, except that the protein value of 10.7g/100g is anomalously high for this food category (typical range 0.5–2.0g/100g) and is likely a data entry error in the HPB source record. The HPB values are preserved as-is per source policy. Secondary nutrients (sodium, fibre, sugar) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the closest approved source. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source record for this preparation; vinegar-pickled vegetables with no added oil plausibly contain negligible fat. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero given fat_g is 0.0. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a plant-based pickled vegetable product. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB source or USDA FoodData Central for this specific pickled vegetable composite as of 2026. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB source record and not available for a directly matching tsukemono composite in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 3. FOOD GROUP CORRECTION: Input record incorrectly classified this food under 'Meat and alternatives / Beef'. Corrected to 'Vegetables / Pickled and fermented vegetables' based on food composition. 4. PROTEIN ANOMALY: Input HPB protein value of 10.7g/100g is anomalously high for pickled vegetables (USDA SR Legacy reports ~0.9g/100g for mixed pickled vegetables). This value is preserved from the HPB source but flagged as a likely data entry error. Downstream users should verify against the original HPB Foodo database entry. 5. SODIUM ESTIMATE: Sodium of 800mg/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for mixed pickled vegetables, consistent with the health_suitability caution in the input record. HPB source did not report sodium. 6. GI NOTE: GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct tsukemono GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per 30g serving = (32 × 2.36 / 100) × 0.30 × 100 ≈ 0.23, rounded to 0.2.
